label: ROUGE
definition: A family of recall-oriented overlap metrics for automatic summarization that scores a generated summary against references by shared n-grams or longest common subsequence; higher is better. It flips BLEU's emphasis from precision to recall because summarization's core risk is omission, but it shares the surface-overlap blind spot: it cannot credit a faithful paraphrase or penalize a fluent factual error.

body.text: ROUGE, the Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation, is the summarization counterpart to bleu-score. Introduced in 2004, it measures how much of a reference summary's content a machine-generated summary manages to cover. Where BLEU asks "how much of what the model wrote is correct," ROUGE flips the emphasis to "how much of what should have been said did the model actually say," which is the right question for summarization, where the danger is leaving important content out.

ROUGE matters because summarization, like translation, was expensive to evaluate by hand, and the field needed a fast automatic proxy to iterate on. ROUGE supplied it and became the near-universal reporting standard for summarization benchmarks such as CNN/DailyMail and XSum. Its recall orientation made it the natural choice for a task whose core risk is omission, and its several variants let researchers probe different notions of overlap, from exact word matches to looser sequence similarity.

The family has a few members. ROUGE-N counts overlapping n-grams, ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-2 (unigrams and bigrams) being the most reported. ROUGE-L uses the longest common subsequence between candidate and reference, rewarding words that appear in the same relative order without requiring them to be contiguous. Each is computed as a recall (fraction of reference n-grams found in the candidate), and modern practice usually reports the f1-score that balances that recall against precision, so that a summary cannot win simply by being long and including everything. This explicit recall term is the structural difference from BLEU, which folds the same concern into a brevity penalty instead.

ROUGE inherits all the weaknesses of surface overlap and adds a few of its own. It cannot credit a faithful abstractive summary that conveys the reference's meaning in entirely different words, and it cannot penalize a summary that copies reference phrasing while introducing a factual error or hallucination, because it does not check faithfulness at all. High ROUGE correlates with extractive, lexically conservative summaries, which is part of why models tuned to maximize it can produce summaries that score well yet read poorly and occasionally misstate the source. These gaps drove the field toward embedding-based metrics like BERTScore, faithfulness-specific checks, and llm-as-judge evaluation.

ROUGE is best understood as the recall-leaning sibling of BLEU within the n-gram overlap family, an applied instance of precision-and-recall and the f1-score for text, and a complement to intrinsic measures like perplexity. Its blindness to paraphrase and to factual correctness is exactly the limitation that semantic and judge-based summarization metrics were created to overcome.
